content.full_text:
A76-26-061

June 18, 1976

Mr. Ken Corkhill
Executiva Director
Independent Auto Dealers
Association of California
2500 Vallejo Street, Suite 105
Santa Rosa, California 95405

Dear Mr. Corkhill:

This letter is to confirm my advice to you by telephone concerning
the filing obligations of the Independent Auto Dealers Association
of California under the Political Reform Act.

On June 4, 1976, you stated the following facts: (1) The
Association's political action committee had $720 on hand as of
January 1, 1976. (2) The political action committee has collected
$170 during 1976 for political purposes. (3) During 1976 the
committee has purchased tickets totaling $240 to testimonial dinners
given for candidates in the June 1976 primary. You asked whether
the Association's political action committee should file campaign
statements pursuant to Government Code Section 84200 in connection
with the primary election. I advised that the political action
committee is not required to file campaign statements regarding
the primary election because it has not collected $300 or more since
January 1, 1976 for political purposes, has not made expenditures
totaling $500 or more during 1976 and has not made monetary contri-
butions of $5,000 or more out of funds on hand as of January 1, 1976.

On June 7, 1976, I advised you that monetary contributions made
directly to candidates and committees are not expenditures for
purposes of determining whether a person qualifies as a committee
under Government Code Section 52013(b).

If you have further questions, please contact me.

Sincerely,


NAN HAMBLETON
Special Compliance Representative

NH:mak

bcc: Jeanne

Independent Auto Dealers Association of California

2500 VALLEJO STREET, SUITE 106, P.O. BOX 2555, SANTA ROSA, CALIF. 95405 AREA CODE 707 - 544-6590

JUN 10 12 13 PM '76

June 7, 1976

Nan Hambleton
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street Bldg.
Sacramento, Ca. 95814

Dear Nan:

Confirming our recent phone call conversations:

On the afternoon of June 4th you advised me that our Political Action Committee would not have to make pre-election and post-election campaign statements until we have received $500 in a calendar year and that we may disburse the small balance without having to make the above reports.

You further clarified this phone conversation when returning my call Monday, June 7th, with the following definition: "...monetary contributions are not considered an expenditure". I understand an application of this definition could be as follows:
If our PAC spent money to influence voters to vote for a particular candidate - that would be an expenditure - but, a monetary contribution, such as attending a testimonial dinner, would not.

I would appreciate confirmation of the above understandings for record keeping purposes.

Sincerely,

Ken Corkhill
Executive Director

KC/jd
cc: Tom Kent, IADAC President
    Members of IADA-PAC

P.S. In your opinion, will our PAC be required to file an "off-year" statement for the period January 1, 1976 to June 30, 1976 if we have not received $500 or more in that period or made "expenditures" (according to the above definition) in that same period?

AUTO DEALERS WORKING TOGETHER TO BETTER THE INDUSTRY

sections.question: Whether the Association's political action committee should file campaign statements pursuant to Government Code Section 84200 in connection with the primary election, given that the committee had $720 on hand as of January 1, 1976, collected $170 during 1976 for political purposes, and purchased tickets totaling $240 to testimonial dinners for candidates in the June 1976 primary. Additionally, whether monetary contributions made directly to candidates and committees are expenditures for purposes of determining whether a person qualifies as a committee under Government Code Section 82013(b), and whether the PAC is required to file an 'off-year' statement for the period January 1, 1976 to June 30, 1976 if it has not received $500 or more in that period.
sections.conclusion: The political action committee is not required to file campaign statements regarding the primary election because it has not collected $500 or more since January 1, 1976 for political purposes, has not made expenditures totaling $500 or more during 1976 and has not made monetary contributions of $5,000 or more out of funds on hand as of January 1, 1976. Monetary contributions made directly to candidates and committees are not expenditures for purposes of determining whether a person qualifies as a committee under Government Code Section 82013(b).
sections.question_synthetic: The Independent Auto Dealers Association's PAC asks whether it must file campaign statements for the primary election given its financial activity, and whether monetary contributions to candidates constitute reportable expenditures.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The PAC is not required to file campaign statements because it has not met the $500 threshold for collections, and monetary contributions directly to candidates are not counted as expenditures for filing requirement purposes.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter from Ken Corkhill (June 7, 1976) and the FPPC response letter from Nan Hambleton (June 18, 1976). The response letter confirms prior telephone advice and answers the specific filing requirement questions. The P.S. question about off-year statement filing appears to be unanswered in the response provided.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the Association's political action committee should file campaign statements pursuant to Government Code Section 84200 in connection with the primary election, given that the committee had $720 on hand as of January 1, 1976, collected $170 during 1976 for political purposes, and purchased tickets totaling $240 to testimonial dinners for candidates in the June 1976 primary. Additionally, whether monetary contributions made directly to candidates and committees are expenditures for purposes of determining whether a person qualifies as a committee under Government Code Section 82013(b), and whether the PAC is required to file an 'off-year' statement for the period January 1, 1976 to June 30, 1976 if it has not received $500 or more in that period.

CONCLUSION: The political action committee is not required to file campaign statements regarding the primary election because it has not collected $500 or more since January 1, 1976 for political purposes, has not made expenditures totaling $500 or more during 1976 and has not made monetary contributions of $5,000 or more out of funds on hand as of January 1, 1976. Monetary contributions made directly to candidates and committees are not expenditures for purposes of determining whether a person qualifies as a committee under Government Code Section 82013(b).

embedding.summary: FPPC Special Compliance Representative Nan Hambleton advises the Independent Auto Dealers Association's PAC that it is not required to file campaign statements for the primary election because its financial activity has not met the statutory thresholds, and clarifies that direct monetary contributions to candidates are not considered expenditures under the Political Reform Act.
